data.title: Did You Survive The Great Crash Of 2025?
data.description:
Listen here or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Daily Stock Picks' Gary Vaughan returns to discuss surviving the 'Great Crash of 2025. (1:00). How Gary uses fundamentals to pick his stocks (5:00). Tesla stock and Elon Musk (10:00). Investing around the tariff conversation (15:30). S&P 500 analysis (18:15). On owning Bitcoin (20:00). Tech ETFs vs stocks (25:25). Top stock picks (27:50).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Gary Vaughan, our friend, has returned from Daily Stock Picks. Great to see you. Great to talk to you as always. Thanks for joining us.

Gary Vaughan: Shouldn't there be, like, triumphant music when I enter here?

RS: I just assume everybody's hearing that in their minds.

GV: Well, I'm just checking in on all my friends if they survive the great crash of 2025 because we're a month later right now. It's May 20. And it seems like if you panicked, you made a mistake.

Steve Cress wrote the greatest, during this time, he wrote one of the best Alpha Picks newsletters was, hey, it's going to outperform on the rebound. And so far, 100%. I'm not a small cap guy. I'm a large cap guy.

He has been right. I've looked at the performance of Alpha Picks. I've got 6 figures into that portfolio. I'm still tracking my, do I want to actually pick and choose Alpha Picks, or do I wanna do the whole portfolio? So far, I've done both.

I did far better in the whole portfolio than I did in just picking and choosing. So kudos to Mister Cress.

RS: I like it. I'd like to hear what names have treated you well out of the those picks. But I'm curious maybe if we could start - all of this market volatility, all of this parsing out of information and true things and fake things and not yet true things and not yet fake things, how have you been approaching these last few months?

Have you been dissuaded from your thesis? Have you doubted yourself? How have you picked yourself back up? Have you been true to your North Star the whole time? Talk us through the last, like, I don't know, six months, few months.

GV: So I am a large cap tech investor. That's the way I've made my money. I just did a Fidelity retirement analysis of my portfolio, which says I'm going to die at 90 with more money than anybody should ever have in a lifetime.

And I think that's mainly due because of the Monte Carlo analysis and they, you know, I'm fairly risky. The number one stock in my portfolio is Apple (AAPL). I've used Apple for the last twenty five years as my savings account.

Hasn't worked out this year because I'm still down about 8%, in my total portfolio. But I think Apple's down around 12% for the year, something of that sort. I sold a bunch in December at $260. I had to pay 20% capital long term capital gains, which my accountant yelled at me for. I didn't plan correctly, but I said, hey. I'm still up.

The 5% that I pay in taxes, I'm still up because at $260, I can always buy it back at 200. So I really haven't done well personally, but I think the plan that I have has worked out. I use both fundamentals and I use technicals.

So my technicals are in TrendSpider, and everybody on my podcast knows I have a four hour algorithm that I bought, which is based on just technicals. And then I use Seeking Alpha as my fundamental platform. And I've loved it.

I will say that in my mind, and I've been saying this for about the last month or so, I think we're range bound on the S&P. And the reason is when you look forward, it's about $300 per earnings for earnings in 2026. That's, I think, what the estimates are telling us.

I think the most recent came in at about 70 or 80, somewhere in that neighborhood. I think it's closer to 70, which would put us at $280 for this year. If you just do the multiple, Jamie Dimon just said yesterday that the multiple of 21 is too high.

He wants it to come down. So in my mind, when I look at the technicals, I think you're between 5 and 6 thousand. Could it go up from there? Absolutely. I think it was yesterday that that that in the first three hours of trading, $4,000,000,000 of retail trading took place. And I think 60% of that was in single stocks, and people bought Tesla (TSLA) and Palantir (PLTR).

Now personally, from a personal standpoint, I'm a guy who sold Palantir at $25. I didn't believe in the fundamentals. I used Seeking Alpha to look at it, and I said, okay, the fundamentals just don't make sense to me.

And I missed out on that whole run.

RS: Let me let me ask you for a second, the fundamentals - lay out what that means to you when you talk about looking at the fundamentals.

GV: So I use PE and I use forward PE. And since talking with Steve Cress on my podcast, he says that the quant loves PEG, which includes growth, the price to earnings to growth.

I use those three metrics from the fundamental standpoint to try and determine, okay, is it a good opportunity? I am 54 years old. At 20 years old, I was fine paying 80 times forward on a stock like, you know, Palantir when it was at 80 and 90. I'm not that guy anymore. At 54, I feel like I have to protect my capital a little bit more.

I've got quite a bit of of portfolio that I need to protect. Now, it probably would have been smart to me to put $10, 15, $20,000 into Palantir and just let it grow.

But at that at that range, it's gonna take them a long time to grow into it, and I think there's better names out there that you could buy in the software space.

For instance, Applovin (APP). It's not, you know, it's not apples to apples. This isn't, you know, it's just software. But Applovin is a great one that I think has pulled back enough, and it's not at its fifty two week high, and provides the opportunity for you to grow.

It's kinda like NVIDIA (NVDA). I know the quant hates NVIDIA, and everybody's like, when's it gonna be a strong buy? It just hates NVIDIA. And and for me, NVIDIA, it's a $3,000,000,000,000 company.

There's only two other companies, Apple and Microsoft (MSFT), that are 3,000,000,000,000. I don't think the market is going to let it become a $6,000,000,000,000 company. So I don't think that the stock goes from $120 to $240, but it could go from $120 to $160. And so the risk reward for that is I'm willing to put a ton of money into NVIDIA because I do think that they're rebuilding the data centers.

And I think when you saw Jensen Huang say this and and I told everybody this morning, Jensen Huang was yelled at yesterday about Taiwan Semi (TSM) raising their prices. And he said, we'll pay whatever we have to pay. And that's bullish for Taiwan Semi. The only risk that you have to the reward on Taiwan Semi is political.

And I think Trump is negotiating with them on the tariff. I don't think we've seen the 10% tariff run its course through the consumer yet. So I think later this year, you're gonna see a couple of things.

I think you're gonna see the consumer become probably a little bit weaker if we do keep these tariffs on, which is going to affect earnings. But I also think you see some deregulation. And that's why I'm big on the financials.

And I am not a financial guy. I am a guy that, essentially, I wrote a Warren Buffett screener. About a month ago, I wrote a Warren Buffett screener, because who doesn't wanna follow Warren Buffett? And I used it. It found a few stocks.

And Wells Fargo (WFC) was one of the stocks. And then I did another Seeking Alpha screener based on the PEG ratio that Steve Cress told me he loved. It found four stocks.

It found Uber (UBER). It found Gilead (GILD), which I'm not a medical guy, so I said, okay. I'm not buying that one. Found Bank of America (BAC) and found Wells Fargo. All of that led me to, hey. I'm gonna buy more Bank of America under 40. I'm gonna buy Wells Fargo under 50. And I'm gonna buy Uber under 80. And so far so good.

I mean, all of those three stocks, they've done great, and I found them with the Seeking Alpha Screener. So, again, I think it's an interesting kind of play about trying to find value in this market because we do have high multiples, but you're gonna pay for it for industry leaders.

Tesla's a great example. Am I comfortable paying 45 times forward earnings, I think is what it is, for a car company? No. But for an AI company that also integrates energy and and has some type of ability to actually go into a robotics, area, which is a $3,000,000,000,000 industry alone, I think that again, that's risk reward.

So I think from a macro level, you've really got to find your spots. And I told people, if you got a $10,000 portfolio, I think 20% of that today should be in risk assets, meaning, you know, Bitcoin (BTC-USD). I called it at the beginning of the year, I said Bitcoin's gonna touch 150,000.

So at 100,000, put 1% of your portfolio into it. Because we all know you lost way more than 1% during that downturn in your portfolio of just Liberation Day. If you had 1% in the the Bitcoin or risk assets, you don't wouldn't even know if it went to zero. And I think Bitcoin is highly unlikely to go to zero. Is it possible? Absolutely.

We've seen things like that happen. But I think it's you know, again, find your stocks. Find your comfort level. My comfort level is both technicals and fundamentals, and I go over every morning on my podcast.

And so people I told people my podcast is more me talking my own book, because it was the one hour in the morning that I used to take even with a full time job looking at my portfolio, making sure that I was on top of things, and I could do an elevator pitch for every stock in my portfolio.

RS: You mentioned Tesla. How do you factor in the Musk angle? How do you quantify that?

GV: If he would get out of politics, I'd be happier. I bought a Model 3 in 2018. And it's funny, my buddy, Clint bought the same Model 3. I think he bought a Performance, I bought a regular. He loved the car so much, he put in his entire portfolio into Tesla alone. He is far wealthier than I am today. I did not have that.

I actually traded around Tesla. I think the Musk angle, he is necessary for that company to continue to grow. I don't think that taking him out of that business does anybody any good. He has shown. He has the ability to build a company. I I don't necessarily agree with his politics.

I don't necessarily sign on for his politics. I am completely middle of the road politically. But the reality of it is that man has the ability to look at a business and see five to ten years forward.

Whereas everybody else, I think, in building businesses maybe has a two year outlook. But he's able to say, you know, we're gonna do this and we're gonna do it right. And I think the robotics, I'm not a huge fan of the autopilot stuff, although I will tell you I had the autopilot drive me to Lowe's the other day, just just this past weekend. It's very impressive. It's awesome.

Two months ago, it wasn't. So the scale of what they're doing and they're changing things, it's super - do I still trust it to fall asleep at the wheel on a local road? No. Do I trust it enough to fall asleep on a highway? Probably, but we'll just leave that there.

RS: So that's your questioning of it, the efficacy right now as as it stands today?

GV: He's got the ability to take that software and, remember, it's all cameras. So if he has the ability to train an AI just based on cameras and to actually have an ability I will tell you on my drive to Lowe's the other day, there was a bird in the middle of the road eating roadkill. It pulled it pulled across the yellow line and avoided the bird. It it was it wasn't a huge bird. It was a tiny little bird.

So the reality of that is there are so many other things that you can take that software and do.

I don't think that Tesla is a car company. I think they're going to be able to take that software. And he showed off, I think he said and I'm not an electrician, so forgive me if I'm wrong. But he's got a lot of need for power for AI. Well, what's a 40% of of the the the profit margin for for Tesla? It's power. So they're a power company as well. Add in the power, the robotics, the AI, and then the car company as a side business.

I think you've got the ability to actually grow. I'm not a Cathy Woods guy who's saying it's going to $4,000 or whatever her current price target is. But I am a guy who says, hey, at $300, it could go to 6. I think it just passed $1,000,000,000,000 in market cap. I think with $3,000,000,000,000 in the robotic business, I think that could easily go to a $2,000,000,000,000 company.

RS: And even if Musk stays on in his political role, you don't think that political isn't the spite that cuts off his nose, let's say, to mix our metaphors?

GV: I don't think so. I think in my mind, I've always said, I lean Democrat for social stances, but probably conservative for financial purposes. But the reality of him doing things, I think it hurts the brand, but I don't think it hurts the actual products.

And when I say that, I think he's going to be able to bring a car to market that costs 20 to $30,000, and he's got the benefit of being 100% US made in this country. And if he can do that, he fends off the Chinese electric comp car companies with self driving.

I mean, make no mistake. BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) has a full car that is far less than a Tesla, and it has full self driving in China. And it's just a matter of time before that technology and that that car company starts creeping into America. I think it's already one of the number one car companies in South America because it's so cheap.

So I think he's going to be able to fend that off. Now, will he take on, gas cars in this country? That's a whole another story. I think there there needs to be some some pretty historic change, because I do, like, for instance, I drive up to New Jersey a lot from Atlanta, and I think it takes six stops for me in a decent decent amount.

So it takes about fifteen hours instead of twelve hours in a gas car. But listen, I'm 54 years old. I gotta make those stops anyway. So I do think I get a lot of questions from my friends, you know. Have I gotten, any feedback, any retaliation against my car here in Atlanta? I haven't. I haven't seen it. I think there's a large news cycle about that, but we've seen the the deliveries of model 3, the electric cars from Tesla, and they have gone down.

So it's a real thing to worry about. I just think that the news cycle is quick, and I think it will forget that with a price price reduction.

RS: You mentioned the tariff conversation. How are you paying attention to that along the way? What are the markers that you're noticing along the way?

Are you focused on earnings calls as they start to come out in the coming months as as ways to see what's going on there. How are you thinking about that and leveraging it?

GV: So I'm not an expert in this. And I will completely tell you, I have been a trade expert. I have been a foreign policy expert, and now I'm a bond expert. I think from a tariff standpoint. I like what I heard from and I forget the economist that told me, but 4.5 is the bond rate. Under 4.5%, I think I wanna buy. Under over 4.5, I think you gotta look at the VIX and see where the volatility index is.

Because we've seen that spike with some of the news that comes out from this administration. So the tariffs, I don't think 10% tariffs have made its way into the marketplace.

And like I said, I think they're going to hurt earnings. But I think that negotiation ability of the Trump administration, I think they don't want to do what they did on Liberation Day.

What they their plan was on Liberation Day was to actually bring the bond market down. It worked for a time. But when you saw it creep back up to 4.5, I think it set panic into Bessent and the Fed.

And so I think that's the key to trying to decide, okay, should I go in or shouldn't I go in? At 4.5%, I'm I'm more patient than I am buying. Like, you know, and I'm talking ETFs like (VOO) and (QQQ) and things like that.

If you've got an opportunity to get into a stock, I do think a year from now, we are higher than we are today on many of the stocks in my, Daily Stock Pick Core portfolio.

So I think you're you're fine buying some of those. But I think from a standpoint of buying for the long term, like an index fund, I think I wanna buy it when it's under 4.5 and the VIX is under 20. And I just say that as the VIX gets up towards 50 and and the bond market is at 4.6, 4.7, that may be where I actually wanna start buying, just to try and buy the dip because we saw that. I think you're seeing it's a buy the dip market from a retail perspective.

I think the big money's still on the sidelines, and they don't know what to do because I think there is a Liberation Day 2.0 that is coming at some point in time this summer.

And then I think you start to see, really them really focus on the the the midterm elections and the economy as a whole. They don't wanna go into those midterm elections with the the S&P (SP500) at 5,000. They wanna go into those midterm elections with the S&P at 6,000.

RS: What do you have a sense of where the S&P lands at the end of the year? Do you give thought to that far down the line?

GV: You know, it's funny because I heard a great, and I it may have just been me talking to somebody, but any true analyst that is worth their weight would just literally take the beginning of the year and add 8% and say that's where I think the S&P is going.

And I haven't mapped it out, but that's where I would say we end the year at. You can't have two years of 23,20 percent.

If I told you you have three time periods. One year, you're down 10%. One year, you're up 20%. One year, you're up 23%. And and we don't know what the next year was. But the next year, I would say, would be a a down year or an even year or even an average year. I'd still want to invest in that market four years ago. So in my mind, I like to say that that we're gonna be at least 8% higher on the average than where we started.

I do think you end up higher than where you are today, and I haven't necessarily looked at it today. But I'm still bullish. I think today my portfolio I think is about 20% cash, which I don't feel comfortable with.

But with the volatility that we're seeing, I do feel comfortable with it. I particularly like to be historically about 95% invested. And that's the way I just like my money working for me. And it's always been stocks.

I've never been a bonds guy. I've never bought a bond. I've never owned a bond. It's always been stocks. And for my career in investing, which goes about thirty five close to forty years, I've just liked stocks.

RS: So speaking of stocks, let's talk about funds, which you were just talking about index funds, but you were talking about Bitcoin before.

I'm curious how you think about owning Bitcoin, how you encourage your average retail investor, maybe you're also your non average retail investor, how to play with Bitcoin.

GV: So I'm older. I'm 54, like I said. And that's gonna be a theme. I don't even know how to buy Bitcoin other than through Robinhood, Venmo, or something like that. And I know the fees and the prices are just outrageous. So my personal way to do it is I just buy (IBIT).

It's one of the largest, largest Bitcoin funds out there, and you can go and look at any of the ones that you like. It's got some of the lowest fees. Literally, under 50, I wanna buy it. Over 50, I just wanna sit there and hold it.

But it's a long term buy and hold. My plan is to hold that for five to ten years, maybe a year. If we dip down below 50,000 on Bitcoin, maybe I wanna start taking some profits on that after a year.

But the reality is I want to buy and hold that. It's never more than 1% of my actual portfolio. So I never invest more than 1%. It could grow to 10%, but I never invest more than 1% of my actual total portfolio.

And that's what I tell the average retailer to do. If you know how to buy crypto and you know crypto in fact, I'm in a high net worth group where they actually offered, crypto funds.

So it's hedge funds that actually trade crypto, and their returns are insane. There are people making just bank on these hedge funds that are trading crypto in very large quantities.

I didn't feel comfortable with it. I didn't take it. I didn't take the investment. I just wound up, you know, buying IBIT, and I'm perfectly happy with that.

And if you're not happy with it, go and find a risk asset that you're happy with. Maybe it's gold. You know, gold is considered a store of wealth, but we've seen that 30% this year.

And so, you know, I said with the debt the way that it is, and with the downgrade from Moody's, I think gold is a decent investment, even though you're at all time highs. I took gold in February. I think I played, Newmont, (NEM) as the symbol.

And I sold out of it a couple of weeks ago. I said, you know what? I'm just gonna take my profits. It was a technical play. It was under its 200 weekly moving average. And I said the strong move in gold is probably gonna take it up to its 200 weekly.

And I didn't play (GLD). I probably would have been better playing GLD. But I just took that as a technical trade. And once it got through there, the thesis was done and I got out.

RS: And IBIT, you would say, is that mostly because of brand name recognition that you got into them?

GV: Yeah. I think Fidelity. Fidelity is my broker. They want me to go with the Fidelity one, but I honestly, I don't even know the symbol (FBTC).

And I just remember IBIT. So when I think about Bitcoin or somebody mentions Bitcoin, and then I look at how much I actually own, and I decide, maybe I want a little bit more.

I just literally put IBIT into the app. And wherever I'm at, if somebody says Bitcoin's going up, I look at my allocation to it. How I've actually invested. And if it's not at 1%, I start adding more. And if it's under 50 If IBIT's under 50, and that's just that's the current price that I'm looking at.

So if you're listening to this two years from now, do not quote me that it's way over 50 because I think it might be way over 50. But again, 50 in the during this time period is where I feel comfortable buying.

RS: And do you have a price target on Bitcoin? Is that something you think about?

GV: I think this year, we touch $150k. I think that's realistic. I think you've got you've got a president in Trump who actually owns an exchange.

He's got a couple of meme coins, which, coming into this year you didn't even know about. But at the Bitcoin summit, I guess, in January or something, he had meme coins rolled out there.

So I think you've got a a very friendly administration to crypto. And, I think it was Howard Lutnick that says, anybody who's selling Bitcoin, he's the one buying. This guy's got billions in bit and he's in the administration.

So I think you touch 150. I've told people if you invest at 100, expect it to go to 150. And at that point, maybe it goes to 200, but I think it's a simple version of supply and demand.

You've got a limited amount of supply of Bitcoin, but you've got new demand that's coming in. JPMorgan, I think Dimon said he won't own it personally, but they're actually housing it at JPMorgan.

So if there's a crypto if there's some type of Federal Reserve with Bitcoin, I think that's just bullish. I think you're just in a time where Bitcoin is bullish. It's not gonna be forever.

People will realize, hey, you know, it's it's not really worth that much, and there's probably going to be a better crypto out there that trades more efficiently, that, uses less power, that actually has more supply and more liquidity.

But I think until that happens, I think Bitcoin's it. And MicroStrategy (MSTR), I just brought that up this morning. My four hour algorithm in TrendSpider makes like 2000% over the past two years versus MicroStrategy's is at 1000%. And not only is Howard Lutnick buying all the Bitcoin, but Michael Saylor's buying all the Bitcoin.

So I do think it's worth the risk. Again, risk reward is very much in my mentality, and I think the risk is quite a bit lower than the actual reward that could come.

RS: The risk reward stock versus ETF conversation, I'll extend it to the tech side. Is it a notion of the reward is much higher than the risk in terms of going after the stocks? There's just much more upside as opposed to getting into a tech ETF?

GV: I own ETFs. I own (XLK), for the tech sector. So I do own quite a bit of ETFs. I like ETFs to spread out my, exposure. I tell my young listeners that I don't know that you wanna be heavy ETFs when you're actually picking winners and losers because you do want that risk factor of a single stock.

As long as you keep track of it. You know, I got very lucky holding, Apple for twenty five years. I had to white knuckle through, I think, three or four different 40 to 50% downturns in that stock.

And I got lucky that it continued up, but the thesis had had never changed. I still bought every new product. I still owned every, service that they had. So again, the thesis hadn't changed.

Now that it's 30 times forward earnings and their actual revenues not growing as much, and they've fallen behind on AI, and I have an iPhone 13 Pro, not a 16 Pro, maybe it's time to start trimming. And that's why I started trimming in December for the first time. I think 2022 was the first time I actually sold Apple since 2004.

So again, the thesis has semi changed and the position in my portfolio. I was on with Dan Niles one time, and he's a big bear of Apple. And I said, Dan, give me one reason why I should hold Apple. And he said he couldn't. And he went through all the fundamentals of the stock.

And he said, listen, you know, we're all on iPhones. We're all on that. But the actual, new products that they're rolling out, it doesn't force you to buy new stuff. So, again, maybe the thesis has changed.

So I do think that ETF spread that risk, but I think you still it depends on your age and your your your actual goals. If your goals are to make as much money as you can, I think ETFs are not the way to go?

Probably single stocks would be the way to go. If your, goal is to protect the money that you have, spreading yourself and diversifying yourself with ETFs is absolutely the way to go.

RS: And what would you say, hearkening back to the beginning of the conversation, about highlighting some alpha picks and or other some of your top picks out there?

GV: Well, I'll tell you. One of my listeners, actually wrote me this weekend. About a month ago, I was on your top stocks. And I just scroll, and if people wanna see it, sometimes I'll scroll over to the top stocks while I'm kinda goofing around with the audience before I record or after I record the podcast on YouTube.

And I just glanced at this stock, (PRCH). I loved the AI analysis. You guys rolled out that AI analysis, and I love it. I'm not a guy that goes into detail in stocks, and I'm more of a Chris Camillo. Chris Camillo, if you know him, he turned like $800,000 into $43,000,000 by what he calls social arbitrage.

And just the other day, just yesterday, actually it was yesterday, he rolled out. He said, hey, this stock, I think it's, I wrote down the symbol. What is it? It's (AS) is this symbol. And I forget the name of the company. But he showed how TikTok, the mentions of Solomon Shoes was in a straight upward pattern on Google Trends.

And their earnings were actually today. And he said, this is a no brainer. And so that's what I like to do. I like to just look at the fundamentals of a stock, see what it is, and then Google some social arbitrage.

Do I use it? Well, this AS, they make Salomon shoes. I'm a hiker. Amer Sports. That's the name. Yeah. I use, I actually wear, Salomon Trail Runners when I hike. I didn't know that was the the stock. And so I just did a little bit of research. Boom. It's up, like, 15% today on their earnings.

And this, again, that kind of explains even that Apple purchase that I did in 2004. Everybody said nobody wants a phone without a keyboard, and then you try it and boom. And I went to my IT department, with the place that I worked, and I said, hey, I want an iPhone. And they said, well, you can't have it. You need a Blackberry because it's unsecure.

And I said, how many people are actually asking for iPhones? And they said, we get about 15 or 20 a day. There were only a few hundred employees at this company. So again, I saw the development of that. I didn't know exactly what I was doing.

But back to this PRCH and the AI. I looked up the top stocks. I loved what the AI analysis said. I added it to my watch list. I didn't take the trade. Now, general watch list, I don't always take the trade, but it's something that I continue to look at.

He wrote to me this week, and he said, hey, I took that trade when you looked at it. I'm up 11% over one month. It just paid for my Seeking Alpha subscription. And there are so many others.

One that I just looked at the other day that I actually like. We know Warren Buffett has a secret stock that he's buying. And so, again, back to my screener of Warren Buffett. I looked at it and I looked at some of these things. One that I actually found that he is not going to buy that I really like right now is, Freshworks. (FRSH), it's an AI company. I don't know too much about it, but damn, it looks really, really good.

And I like the chart. I like that. But we also know that the last time Buffett found a stock that he loved, it was Chubb (CB). It was an insurance company. I looked at your top stocks, and I found this (HRTG). It's an insurance company. It's the only insurance company that I saw with that was actually fairly valued. And it's still way, way up.

This takes me back to just looking at it last year. Last year, I was scanning the top stocks and I found (PSIX). That's up 736%. We found that when that was $4. So many of my listeners did enjoy that one while we were doing it.

I think that the what I like about Seeking Alpha, you can literally go down a rabbit hole. It can take you twenty minutes, and you can buy find 15 or 20 stocks that you actually can add to a watch list, or it could take you four hours to actually just research one of the stocks that you like. It's self guided.

And you guys provide that great interface that allows me to do it. And your tools and the charting and stuff like that, even if somebody's not a technical trader and they don't wanna look at technicals, it's super simple.

Daniel Snyder did a great job of guiding people on how to use those charts and stuff. So again, kudos to to the platform because for so many years, I used free tools like Finviz, and and you don't need a paid subscription to actually make money.

But the fact that I don't get pop ups anymore, and I can just go to one place and say, okay, I like Apple. Let me go and find some stocks like Apple. You know, what is Apple like today? Let me go and create a screener. And then well, how has this performed against Apple during this time? So, again, the tools that you guys provide are just top notch, and I love it.

RS: Well, I really appreciate that. The Daniel Snyder video that Gary is talking about for those that don't know, we have webinars explaining various things. Daniel Snyder does a great job laying out how to best use Seeking Alpha for your portfolio for the best value that you can get it for, which is not to leave out Gary's excellent analysis and channel.

You have a podcast. You have a daily livestream, Daily Stock Picks. A lot to get into there. Gary's as entertaining as he is here, probably a lot more so because he's got the video component going.

data.title: Niagen Bioscience: Overbought After Rally
data.description:
Today, I put Niagen Bioscience, Inc. (NASDAQ:NAGE) in the spotlight. The last time I looked at this company was in 2022 when it was known as ChromaDex Corporation and its flagship product was a proprietary form of nicotinamide riboside branded as Tru Niagen. The firm changed its name in March of this year, and the stock has had a nice run in 2025 under its new moniker. Can the good times continue? An analysis follows below.

Niagen Bioscience, Inc. is headquartered in Los Angeles, CA. The company operates out of three main business segments: Consumer products; Ingredients; and Analytical Reference Standards and Services. The stock currently trades around ten bucks a share and sports an approximate $800 million market capitalization.

The company's focus is developing products based on nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide or NAD+. Management's vision is based on this focus, and it describes NAD+ on its website in the following way:

AD+ is an essential coenzyme, present in all living cells, and is fundamental to mitochondrial bioenergetics, metabolic homeostasis, and genomic stability. Research shows NAD+ levels decline with age and metabolic stress, correlating with diminished cellular function and physiological resilience.

The company sells through distributors as well as direct to consumer and Ecommerce makes up approximately 55% of overall sales. The two biggest contributors to sales are Tru Niagen and Total Niagen ingredient sales. The latter business to supply food grade and pharmaceutical grade Niagen. The company has primarily a US-based supply chain and leadership has stated it currently sees little potential tariff impacts on its overall business.

The company is also developing Niagen Plus (an IV version of Niagen). A phase 3 study evaluated it to help treat Parkinson's disease should conclude shortly and initial results out before the end of this year. Management is also in an ongoing dialogue with the FDA about NR for the potential treatment of ataxia-telangiectasia. Leadership has pledged to give an update on any progress on this front sometime this year.

Recent Results:

Niagen Biosciences, Inc. posted its Q1 numbers on May 7th. It was a solid first quarter for the company under its new name. Niagen Biosciences delivered a GAAP profit of six cents a share, triple the two pennies a share consensus. Net Income was $5.1 million. This compares to a net loss of $0.5 million or $(0.01) per share for the first quarter of 2024.

Gross margins increased 270bps from the same period a year ago to $63.4 million. Operating expenses rose $1.4 million but fell 380bps as a percent of sales it should be noted. Adjusted EBITDA came in at $4.9 million, compared to just $700,000 in 1Q2024.

Revenues rose just over 37% on a year-over-year basis to $30.5 million, more than $3 million north of expectations. Sales broke down in the following way (above). Tru Niagen sales rose 24% to $21.5 million, Total Niagen ingredient sales rose 95% to $8 million, and the company's new revenue stream from pharmaceutical sales contributed $1 million. Management boosted its previous FY2025 sales guidance. Leadership now projects sales growth of between 20% to 25%, up from its previous baseline calling for 18% revenue growth.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

The small Wall Street community that follows Niagen Bioscience is positive on the company, but with the recent rise in the stock, the shares are at the consensus price target. Since first quarter results, H.C. Wainwright ($11 price target) and Roth MKM ($10 price target, up from $8 previously) have reissued Buy ratings. In early March, Ladenburg Thalmann ($8.10 price target, up from $6.80 previously) also reiterated its Buy rating. That is the only analyst firm commentary since Niagen Biosciences debuted with its new name.

Approximately three percent of the outstanding float in the shares is currently held short. There has been no insider activity in the stock so far in 2025. Niagen, Inc. ended Q1 with just over $55 million of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet against no long-term debt based on the 10-K the company filed for the quarter. Niagen delivered $7.9 million in free cash flow in the quarter, compared to just $300,000 in 1Q2024. The company saw its cash balances rise by $17 million in FY2024.

Conclusion:

Niagen, Inc. made a nickel a share (GAAP) on $99.6 million in revenues in FY2024. The current analyst firm consensus has profits rising to 12 cents a share as sales rise to $123 million in FY2025. They project earnings to rise 21 cents a share in FY2026 on just over 20% revenue growth.

After its big run, the stock sells for nearly 50 times FY2026E EPS and eight times trailing revenues. That seems an extreme valuation for what is basically an ingredients company. The stock is selling at the top of its three-year price to sales ratio range and is topping most analyst firm price targets. The stock also does not pay a dividend and based on cash generated in FY2024, has an approximate two percent free cash flow yield. With the stock up over 90% YTD, the shares appear significantly in overbought territory. Therefore, this is an equity I would avoid at current trading levels.

data.title: AMD: Positives Everywhere
data.description:
Executive Summary

Buying the Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) dip in late February was a good move as the stock has outperformed the S&P 500 since then. The rally was not quite big, meaning that AMD is still attractively valued with a solid upside potential. AI tailwinds remain intact, and AMD's management continues executing on the growth strategy and expanding its partnership network. I continue buying AMD stock and confirm the Strong Buy rating for it.

AMD Analysis

AMD remains one of the most important semiconductor companies in the world, which are expected to drive the AI revolution. This is evident from the fact that Lisa Su (the CEO) was among the large U.S. presidential delegation (including CEOs of other largest tech companies) that visited oil-rich Middle East states like Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and United Arab Emirates.

This is a very bullish indicator because being recognized at the highest official level speaks volumes about the importance of AMD for the new era of digital revolution. Therefore, I firmly agree with opinions of BofA and Wedbush analysts who mentioned AMD among the largest beneficiaries from deals with aforementioned countries.

This step will help AMD to expand its footprint in an emerging region with the combined GDP of these three countries of circa $1.8 trillion. In addition, GDP per capita across these states is quite high on average, which is another positive factor creating more opportunities for AMD. Overall, after new Middle East deals, Mizuho raised AMD's target price from $117 to $135.

AMD's strategic importance for the entire AI revolution is not just a coincidence but thanks to the company's innovation-focused strategy. The R&D spending curve demonstrates a clear upward trend, and the company's balance sheet is strong enough to boost the innovation budget further. The company's outstanding cash is significantly higher compared to the total debt, which is a perfect setup for fueling new projects.

The company continues consistently rolling out new products and improvements, which is vital to protect its technological edge. Fresh developments included the release of the Adrenalin Edition 25.5.1 driver updates, mostly aimed at delivering performance improvements for the company's latest upscaling technology, FidelityFX Super Resolution 4 (FSR 4). This roll out is crucial to maintain AMD's strategic positioning in the global videogaming industry. Sustaining a competitive edge in the gaming market is vital because the industry is thriving and has potential to deliver a solid 12% CAGR by 2030.

Expanding technological partnership with Microsoft Copilot is another crucial positive development for AMD. The software giant recently brought its first Copilot+ PC features to AMD-based PC devices. This development is huge because Microsoft's Copilot is one of the most advanced generative AI offerings at the moment and it has great potential to massively expand its market reach over time as Microsoft commands a large 21% market share in the global cloud industry.

Building a strong technological bond with one of the most important cloud and generative AI players is another crucial factor in the company's future long-term success because the secular AI shift also affects the global PC industry. According to Canalys, global deliveries of AI-capable PCs will skyrocket in the next few years with a projected 44% CAGR between 2024 and 2028. AMD's strong presence in the industry (Client & Gaming segment) makes the company's chances to capitalize on this secular shift quite high.

Finally, the global data center buildout continues accelerating its momentum. This is another big positive factor for AMD because the company's Data Center segment also demonstrates strong growth. According to the recent report from McKinsey, the global AI race will require several trillions to be invested in AI infrastructure and data centers are expected to represent large portion of this buildout.

Therefore, both external and internal catalysts are quite bullish. The quantity and quality of these catalysts is impressive, and that is why I remain highly bullish on AMD's future prospects.

AMD Looks Extremely Cheap

Due to Nvidia's leadership in the industry, it is reasonable to use this company's valuation ratios as a benchmark. Compared to Nvidia, AMD's valuation ratios look much more attractive, especially looking at the forward P/E ratios for the next three years. As we can see, AMD's forward P/E ratios are significantly lower, indicating attractive valuation. From the perspective of several other ratios as well, AMD looks significantly undervalued.

Consensus of Wall Street analysts forecasts rapid EPS growth for the next few years. As a result, it is fair to apply confident free cash flow growth for AMD's DCF model. According to Wall Street consensus forecast, the bottom line will deliver a 20%+ average growth rate by 2029.

I use AMD's TTM free cash flow to start my DCF, which is $3.355 billion. A 25% CAGR is implemented in line with the projected EPS CAGR (the above table). The discount rate is 8.4%, and the growth rate into perpetuity is 6%. The growth rate of 6% is fair, in my opinion, given AMD's 12.41% average growth rate delivered over the last four decades.

The DCF suggests that there is a 46% upside potential. AMD's cumulative fair value is around $272 billion, significantly larger compared to the current market cap of $186 billion.

Potential Drawbacks

Despite being a very financially strong company with cutting-edge offerings for data centers, AMD is highly unlikely to close the gap with Nvidia in foreseeable future. The primary reason is Nvidia's much larger scale, which gives it a competitive advantage. Larger scale helps in exercising a much stronger operating leverage, which results in unmatched cash flow generating capacity. As a result, Nvidia has much more spare capital to invest in growth and innovation. In a thriving industry like semiconductors, which is powered by massive AI tailwinds, there is enough room for growth for both Nvidia and AMD. However, investors should have realistic expectations about AMD's potential to dethrone Nvidia.

Geopolitical risks should not be discounted either. The recent trade deal between China and the U.S. is for 90 days only, and it is still uncertain whether the deal will be prolonged or no. In addition, we also have to keep in mind that AMD outsources manufacturing to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM). Since there are historical tensions between China and Taiwan, AMD faces geopolitical risks from this side as well.

Deteriorated bullishness from Wall Street analysts is another potential headwind for a robust rally. Consensus rating is still above 4, meaning that the average recommendation is Buy. However, we should not ignore the fact that the average rating has declined notably over the last six months.

The Verdict

AMD's massive upside potential makes this stock a Strong Buy. Despite Nvidia's dominance in the industry, AMD's strategic positioning is also fundamentally strong, and the company is ready to capitalize on AI tailwinds.

data.title: Darden Restaurants, Inc.: Overvalued, Wait For A Better Price
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I’ve covered a few companies in the consumer discretionary restaurant sector, and as some consumer spending has remained resilient, we’ve seen some concerning trends with slower traffic in this sector. Darden Restaurants, Inc. (NYSE:DRI) is a restaurant conglomerate that has been around for almost 60 years who owns and manages well-known restaurant chains like LongHorn Steakhouse and Olive Garden. Based on my fundamental analysis approach, I believe DRI is currently overvalued with a fair value price target range between $156.85 to $160.65. Although I’m concerned about their high debt levels in an uncertain economic environment where costs may increase and apply further pressure to their margins, I believe the company is overall a hold because it has displayed slow growth over time. Their acquisition of Chuy’s, and some innovations in restaurant prototype buildings which have decreased costs should help the company moving forward. However, it is too expensive for me to start an investment, and I will wait for a lower price, closer to my fair value target.

Earnings And Outlook

Last quarter, DRI disappointed, missing revenue and GAAP earnings expectations. Management highlighted how the industry in total had seen a slowdown. Although there was some concern with the overall consumer trends the industry was seeing, the still provided single digit growth in key business segments. They also highlighted a key partnership with their Olive Garden branch and Uber Direct, operated by Uber Technologies, Inc. (UBER), which, I believe, will help them drive revenue for the popular franchise. Further, they mentioned starting the UBER partnership with some of their other chains as well, which we should see an update about in the next earnings call.

As you will see throughout this article, some concerns I have with DRI are their debt level, increasing costs, and tight margins. DRI is focused on opening new restaurants at a reduced capacity that will help drive startup cost reductions for their popular chains Yard House and Cheddar’s while still offering key menu items. We will see additional updates about how this is going in the next quarter, and cost-cutting will be key for this company as they plan to open 60-65 new restaurants over the next year.

Finally, although the purchase of Chuy’s seems to be in the distant past, we have yet to see that business fully implemented into DRI’s current structure. Last quarter, they just announced that the planning phase has concluded and that they will now start to implement the company into their management systems. Transitions not only have increased costs due to the need for staff training, but also through the implementation of new systems, devices, and supply chains, which is the current stage we are in for Chuy’s. For the next earnings call, I will focus on any updates in this transition as I believe Chuy’s could be one of the main drivers of future growth for this company.

Fundamentals

Currently, DRI trades at a trailing-twelve-month GAAP P/E ratio of 23.48, a forward GAAP P/E ratio of 22.44, a trailing-twelve month EV/EBITDA ratio of 17.29, and a forward EV/EBITDA of 16.36 which are all above the sector medians, indicating that DRI is trading at a premium in their sector. Further, DRI is also trading at a price to cash flow of 14.57 over the trailing twelve months and 15.40 on a forward basis, which is higher than I would want for a mature company that I’d want to invest in from a fundamental value approach. Further, Seeking Alpha’s current valuation grade is a D.

As you can see in the chart below, revenue has consistently increased on an annual basis since 2021, but so have overall costs. Although revenue has grown about 63% since 2021, costs have grown about 62% over this time. From a value standpoint, I typically look for companies that have revenue growth far exceeding growth in costs. Additionally, from a value perspective, I look for companies that are providing value to their shareholders and one of the ways this is done is through share repurchases. Since 2022, DRI has bought back 11.98% of the outstanding shares, which has provided additional yield to shareholders over this time. However, investors should pay attention to the value of repurchasing shares compared to the value that could be provided by investing into a new business venture or expanding their restaurants.

Gross Margin Concern

Below I have a chart including DRI’s yearly margins since 2013. Currently, their gross margins are just below 22%, EBITDA margins are almost 16%, and free cash flow, operating, and net are all below 12%. Although DRI is sort of a conglomerate of restaurants with a wide variety of dining options, if we compare them to a peer in the industry that I’ve covered on this platform, The Cheesecake Factory Incorporated (CAKE) currently has gross margins of over 40%. Now the difference here is that CAKE needs to do a better job of cutting costs to increase their net margin, but having gross margins of under 25% for DRI doesn’t leave a lot of room for further cost-cutting if traffic slows or food prices significantly increase. This is one concern I have with DRI given the current economic environment, but they have higher EBITDA, free cash flow, operating, and net margins than CAKE, which I previously rated as undervalued.

ROIC vs WACC

One of the key fundamentals that I look for in a company is their return on invested capital (ROIC) compared to their weighted cost of capital (WACC). As you can see in the chart below, DRI was doing very well in 2016-2019 with their ROIC-WACC ratio but have struggled to return to this since the pandemic. Over the next few quarters, I will focus on their ROIC to see if they are able to produce a higher level of returns given the higher costs. Based on their last earnings call, it seems they are targeting these higher costs by attempting to decrease start-up costs for new restaurants while maintaining their brand images.

High Retained Earnings Return

Below, I have a chart depicting DRI’s return on equity, return on assets, return on capital, return on capital employed, and return on retained earnings. Prior to the pandemic, DRI struggled to provide significant levels of returns across the board. Although the returns exceeded 20% in some cases, it was stagnant. Now, we see return on retained earnings and return on equity both exceeding 40%. I believe all return metrics are trending in the right direction for DRI since the pandemic lows.

Value Per Share Owned

DRI has a large amount of debt per share, which has increased since the pandemic. Luckily, the interest rate on their debt is relatively low, and their capital can be better employed elsewhere. However, this is a risk for DRI if the economy starts to slow down and consumer spending changes. Right now, DRI does not have enough cash on hand to pay off their debt, and if they face a liquidity problem due to a slowing economy, they may have to reissue shares to cover their debt. Further, book value for the company hasn’t really increased since 2013, with only a 19% total change over that time. Although DRI has provided value to shareholders through larger dividends each year, the overall value of shares has not significantly increased as the company has repurchased stock in recent years. This then is concerning that the company has continued to repurchase shares rather than use the capital on higher potential options.

Dividends

There are a lot of investors out there who look for safe companies that pay a good dividend. I also enjoy receiving dividends from my investments, however, with the current risk-free rate of money market funds still above 4%, this is not a main criterion of mine when looking into individual investments. When evaluating DRI, they have a payout ratio of 59.7% with a yield of 2.68% and 3 years of growth. From a dividend perspective, you’re better off waiting for the price of the stock to fall so that your yield is closer to 4%. As you can see from the image below, the current yield is close to the lowest it’s been over the last 10 years, further indicating short-term overvaluation. Further, given the high payout ratio, I would not expect the dividend to continue growing if we head into a recessionary environment.

Price Target

DRI is a mature business that has somewhat stagnated in revenue growth over the last 10-years, with growth in the single digits. Given this, I used two present value equations to find a fair price target for the company given a 9% growth in earnings per share and a 9% growth in free cash flow. This is following the assumption that free cash flow and earnings per share will not outpace overall revenue growth, which has been 8.5% annually over the last 5-years. As you can see in the output below, my price target for fair valuation given the 9% growth over the next 5- years, assuming a 10% discount, and using trailing twelve-month values for free cash flow and diluted earnings per share, I have a target price range of $156.85 to $160.65. Following similar models on this platform, I have built in margins of safety for which I’m comfortable investing in during any market condition. Given only this price target range, this could be considered overvalued. The model is based on my assumptions, and similar to how I’ve priced similar companies in the past. However, if we assume DRI hit’s their target EPS announced in their last earnings call of $9.45-$9.52, the upper range fair value target is closer to $171-$172, which is still below the current stock price.

Risks to Investment Thesis

The main risk I see with this company is their debt level, which has not recovered since the pandemic. Obviously, the pandemic was a black swan event and likely will not happen again in the near future, but with that much debt on the balance sheet, I’m concerned that a significant economic slowdown could impact this company more so than a company with less debt. Further, with the limited cash on hand, it makes it difficult for a company to continue to grow and advance in a rising cost environment, and I would expect their returns on investments to continue to be impacted by rising costs. Finally, another risk to this thesis, given that I believe the company is a hold, they could significantly outperform from their implementation of Chuy’s over the next few quarters, which we have not seen the full impact of yet.

Conclusion

DRI is a conglomerate of different types of restaurants and cuisines that represents an interesting investment in the consumer discretionary restaurant sector if looking for overall restaurant type diversity. However, based on my fundamental analysis above and my fair value price target range given their growth including a required margin of safety of 10%, I believe the company is a hold at this time. DRI is currently a little more expensive than where I would want to initiate a position; however, the fundamentals are not a screaming sell, and they have yet to fully implement Chuy’s which could drive growth for this company. I would like to see higher returns on invested capital and higher returns on their capital than what they are currently achieving. Additionally, I would like to see gross margin improvements. I’m looking forward to seeing how Chuy’s is implemented into their current business structure by the end of the summer, which is when I expect there to be some changes in some of the fundamentals I evaluated above. This article is solely based on my opinions, research about the company, and understanding of the business, and investors should perform their own due diligence before making investment decisions.

data.title: BJ's Wholesale Club: Too Expensive For This Growth
data.description:
BJ's Wholesale Club Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:BJ) stock continues to be a relative powerhouse relative to the market. Retail has been tough, but club stores have enjoyed strength as consumers continue to bargain hunt. BJs also has a solid repurchase program in place, though the valuation is a bit stretched for the pace of growth, Make no mistake, the growth is positive, but we are talking low to mid-single digit revenue gains, and mid-single digit earnings per share gains right now. That is positive, though at 28X FWD, shares are a bit rich. As such, we continue to rate shares quality hold.

New money should consider waiting for a more sizable pullback before entering, perhaps closer to $105. In this column, we discuss the just reported Q1 earnings. Folks, we saw another double-line beat for the company with positive news on comparable sales, and new member adds. And despite a world with tariff anxiety and associated costs, the company reiterated guidance, which was positive. Let us discuss.

BJ's Wholesale Club Q1 top-line figures and margin performance

Sales and margins are key for retailers, and we really watch comparable sales. We view those as the most critical metric for the health of a retailer, followed by the margins. The top-line revenue figure was positive for BJs. Total revenues did increase by 4.6% to $5.03 billion. This was a slight miss against consensus estimates by $160 million. Much of this stemmed from weakness in fuel prices. However, the thing to really focus on is that comps are strong here. Despite the top-line being lighter than expected, this was another quarter of very positive comparable sales.

The comparable sales are impacted by fuel and the pricing. Including gasoline, total comparable club sales were up by 1.6% year-over-year. If we back out the impact of gasoline sales, comparable sales were up 3.9%. Digitally enabled comparable sales growth through online sales were up 35% year-over-year, too, with comparable growth up 56% on a two-year basis. This is solid growth for the digital sales front. We expect ongoing adoption of this way of members making purchases. Membership continues to grow as well. That is strong. We also saw membership fee income jump by 8.1%, on the back of higher fees and more members. What about margins?

We also saw strength in margins compared to last year. Gross profit increased to $969.5 million from $883.4 million in the year ago quarter, and this comes despite weaker pricing on fuel. Merchandise gross margin rate expanded slightly here. The merchandise gross margin excludes gasoline sales and membership fee income. It rose 30 basis points. On the expense side of the equation, selling and administrative expenses also rose, however, to $760.9 million from $721.8 million a year ago, due to higher labor and rent costs. Most of this though, was because of new store openings, which also increased depreciation expense.

Adjusted EBITDA saw a big lift on these margins, rising 20.9% to $285.8 million versus $236.4 million last year. Net income followed suit, rising to $149.8 million, up from $111.0 million a year ago. On an EPS basis, earnings were better than expected versus estimates by a whopping $0.23, hitting $1.14, way up from $0.83 a year ago. it was helped in part by the repurchase of 55,000 shares in the quarter, for $6.2 million.

Forward view, and a word on tariffs

So, we saw slightly light numbers on the top line, mostly on the back of fuel pricing. Fuel pricing is volatile, so we think investors need to focus on the comparable sales excluding fuel. As we saw, they were quite positive, and the merchandise gross margin rate was up 30 basis points. While merchandise carried in the clubs face tariffs depending on where it sourced, including a 30% tariff on goods from China, and 10% global reciprocal tariffs, management left its guidance unchanged. It is a very positive piece of news, when other retailers are pulling guidance altogether, BJs has reiterated guidance. It still sees comparable sales (excluding volatile fuel) of 2%-3.5%, with adjusted EPS of $4.10 to $4.30. Now, considering shares are $115, shares are trading at 27.4X FWD earnings at the midpoint of guidance. Since the $4.20 midpoint of the guide is only $0.15 above 2024, that is growth of just 3.7% earnings growth.

In our opinion, BJ's Wholesale Club Holdings, Inc. shares are expensive for this growth at these levels. We think a Hold rating is appropriate, and new money should wait for a correction.

data.title: IQI: Discount Narrowing Drives Results On The Back Of Increased Distribution
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Municipal bonds tend to be fairly uneventful investments, which is one of their key selling features; that, and the federally tax-free interest payments they produce as well. In the closed-end fund structure, it does add a couple of additional factors to consider. That includes being leveraged and the discount/premium mechanic, which both can provide different opportunities.

One of the leveraged muni-focused CEFs is the Invesco Quality Municipal Income Trust (NYSE:IQI). The fund trades at a relatively attractive discount, but it is off the wider discount levels it was at in our prior update. Driving the discount to narrow was the substantial distribution increase the fund put into place; however, we'll also be discussing the largest shortfall in distribution coverage that also caused.

IQI Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.77
  • Discount/Premium: -7.06%
  • Distribution Yield: 7.99%
  • Expense Ratio: 0.91%
  • Leverage: 32.79%
  • Managed Assets: $806.22 million
  • Structure: Perpetual

IQI's investment objective is "to provide current income which is exempt from federal income tax." To achieve that objective, they invest in "a diversified portfolio composed substantially of investment-grade tax-exempt municipal securities."

A look at the credit quality with the fund's latest update, and we can see that the fund is overwhelmingly invested in investment-grade issuances, as the investment policy would suggest. There is only a rather small sleeve of 5.80% allocated to what would be considered junk-rated debt.

Looking further into the fund's holdings and the largest allocation is to local general obligation bonds, primarily allocated to California, Texas and New York. This isn't necessarily that unusual, as these are the largest issuers of muni bonds, primarily due to having relatively larger state economies.

An additional benefit for an investor in IQI is that a portion of the distributions paid may be free from state tax obligations as well, for those who reside in states with income taxes.

When including the fund's leverage expenses, we see a total expense ratio come to 2.95%. Leverage increases volatility and the risk of owning this fund; however, it can also result in the potential for enhanced returns.

Narrowing Discount Drives Results

During our last update, which was around two years ago now, the fund's discount has narrowed quite materially. It took around a year before the fund's discount started to narrow off the double-digit level, but that was one of the more attractive characteristics of the fund at that time.

When looking at it over the longer term, this has put the fund's discount back to around its historical average level. That could make it somewhat fairly attractive, but it really is no bargain that it was previously.

That narrowing discount since our last update has helped the fund's overall total returns when compared to the fund's total NAV returns. In addition, it also performed well against the iShares National Muni Bond ETF (MUB).

Overall, MUB is a non-leveraged exchange-traded fund ("ETF"), which can help illustrate relative performance for the diversified muni bond fund space. During this time, neither produced anything that was necessarily brag-worthy, but outsized total returns aren't going to be something that muni funds tend to produce anyway.

Being that it is a leveraged fund, one may have wished to see better overall total NAV returns for taking the additional risk. However, during this higher interest rate environment, leverage costs and management fees generally exceed the yields that munis can pay. That means that during this environment, the added leverage isn't directly resulting in positive income generation due to the negative spread.

On the other hand, the total share price returns were much more attractive, and that did produce outsized returns on a relative basis.

Distribution Bump Drives Discount Narrowing

As we could see above, the fund's discount narrowed rather rapidly, and that wasn't a coincidence.

It was around that time that IQI lifted its distribution by a whopping ~57%. That was after the fund had continually cut its distribution heading into that period. At $0.0631 per month, it sent it back to the highest level in terms of the monthly payout since 2016/2017.

While the discount narrowing is a positive for investors who picked up shares during the double-digit discount slump, there could be some concern now. The fund didn't suddenly see a spike in income generation from its underlying portfolio; instead, they chose to boost the payout specifically to see the fund's discount narrow. That means that the current distribution rate of ~8% isn't being supported entirely.

Based on the latest annual report for the fiscal year-end 2025, net investment income distribution coverage for the 12 months came to around 64%. Coverage would have been a bit lower, but the increased distribution didn't kick in until June 2024.

As the Fed cut 100 basis points in late 2024 and is expected to cut a couple of more times this year, that could see NII coverage improve.

That said, based on the latest quarterly UNII report, coverage is floating at a touch above 61%. That was for the first quarter of 2025, where the first 100 basis points of cuts are already being factored in.

As of their last report, borrowings were through tender option bond trusts ("TOBs") and carried costs at an average interest rate of 4.37%. They also have variable-rate muni term preferred shares outstanding. Those are based on Securities Industry and Financial Markets Association Municipal Swap Index ("SIFMA") plus 1.10%. That worked out to an average annualized dividend rate of 4.25%. Not too far off from the borrowing costs through their TOB.

While that was the borrowing costs of the funds leverage, the 30 day SEC yield for MUB comes to 3.84%. That's a measurement of what MUB has actually received in terms of interest and the yield that works out to from its underlying portfolio. Therefore, we can see why the fund would have been cutting their distribution while short-term rates were rising, increasing their borrowing costs. That's why we saw the fund's NII go from $0.62 in 2021 and gradually decrease to $0.43 in fiscal 2024.

The good news is that SIFMA today is 1.74%, so we would have actually seen that rate drop quite meaningfully to 2.84% for at least a portion of their borrowings. That would suggest that there is some positive spread between the borrowing costs and what the fund is likely able to yield from its muni portfolio. With further cuts from the Fed on their benchmark rate, that would likely result in even lower borrowing rates.

Unfortunately, the other bad news is that the fund has operating expenses, which include the management fee. Those worked out to 0.91%, which would be put on top of the borrowing costs. So we still aren't really seeing a huge benefit from borrowings, but we are starting to get there.

Right now, the biggest benefit of the leverage is that the managers are earning higher fees on the greater AUM. For investors, it is also the potential for upside participation should long-term rates decline, which would see the underlying munis likely appreciate. As is generally the case with muni-focused funds, they carry long maturities with relatively lower yields, which results in them being quite interest rate sensitive as long-duration assets.

The average maturity is 19.22 years for their muni holdings with an option-adjusted duration of 9.93 years. That means that for every 1% change in rates, we should see the fund move nearly 10%—that would be either up or down.

We saw a number of Nuveen funds do this around the same time as well. These moves were more specifically to narrow the underlying discounts that these funds were trading at. Though it took some pressure from activists for this to occur as well.

For IQI, it was Karpus Management that had been building a position. Since then, they have reduced their stake a bit. Unsurprisingly, it was around last year when the fund's discount narrowed meaningfully.

Bank of America (BAC) also had a position that they closed out, but they are only passive investors anyway.

Given the lack of distribution coverage, it isn't going to be too surprising that we see return of capital in the distribution's classification. Not all ROC is negative, but in this case, it is coming from the lack of distribution coverage.

ROC can have its own tax-friendly characteristic as it defers tax obligations, but that is through reducing an investor's cost basis. At some point, that could result in capital gains having to be paid in the future if sold at a gain. So that changes the dynamic of the fund from being primarily tax-free distribution driven to a combination of tax-free and tax-deferred.

Conclusion

We saw a massive increase in the fund's distribution, and that helped to drive the fund's discount to narrow. That in turn also helped to see the fund's total share price results drive higher as well. However, instead of providing a mostly tax-free distribution to its investors, it now comes with a combination of tax-free and tax-deferred distributions. That would be the result of no longer supporting the distribution with the actual income generation from the underlying munis within the portfolio any longer as they overpay.

That said, the good news is that the fund's discount is still at a decent level compared to its historical average. Further, if the Fed cuts its target rate further, the leverage could start contributing positively and result in increased NII. An additional factor is that if long-term risk-free rates also decline, we could see the potential upside from the likelihood of appreciation from those underlying munis in the portfolio.

As we know, this isn't the only muni fund that has now taken this approach do to a wave of funds seeing activist pressure last year. While I think a fixed-income fund covering its payout from income is more attractive, I think that if investors know what they are getting into, it can still be okay to hold. Additionally, the discount overall is still relatively decent for a 'Hold' rating here.

data.title: Merlin Properties: Crudely Around 20% NAV Upside Before Tax Considerations
data.description:
MERLIN Properties SOCIMI, S.A. (OTCPK:MRPRF) is continuing to grow rents and FFO, and the matter of the SOCIMI rule changes seems to be put to bed for the time being with no new mention of it and it not making it into legislation for tax reform. The asset value of their emerging data center business is leading the rent growth contribution figures, which makes sense for an emerging segment that is taking the bulk of their CAPEX commitments. It also means that rent growth is very likely to follow, even just on the existing investments. We think that datacenters continue to be a reasonably interesting area, but we are not entirely convinced that it won't become an industry that will end up in oversupply, considering the real innovation now in AI is going to be on the interfacing with hardware in our opinion, pointing at DeepSeek. On a discount from NAV (net asset value) basis, there is around 20% upside for Merlin. If their ongoing investments are profitable, they are of course capable of growing their GAV (gross asset value) nicely. Quite a lot of their CAPEX is on development, which could return substantially well. Normally, a 20% upside on a NAV discount basis wouldn't be that interesting, but in all likelihood their significant planned CAPEX should generate meaningful value in datacenters. Compared to a typical holding box, Merlin should be able to reinvest (or, given their REIT-equivalent structure, raise and invest) cash as better than average rates.

Latest Earnings

There weren't that many asset rotations in the Q1. Just around 37 million EUR, but the premiums were decent in the low double digits, and ahead of costs of capital in the current environment. Currently, quite a lot of development and acquisitions are on data centers. They are built but not yet yielding, hence the discrepancy between rents and GAV contribution as of the FY. Now, with construction and commercialization risks behind that first wave of assets, there could be some revaluations as discount rates on those assets fall. But current NAV stands at around 8 billion EUR on the 6.7 billion EUR market cap, so an upside of 20%.

Pending CAPEX on the datacenter business is around 2 billion EUR, and a lot of it will be focused on greenfield development, which should yield even better on investment. Current rent growth is 2.7% overall for the business, but just from currently booked GAV of datacenter assets, we should expect growth in rents from datacenters proportional to their GAV contribution in the rent figures as the capacity becomes delivered and rents begin to be earned. The current installed capacity is still at 1/3rd of the expected for Phase I investments, expected to be completed and mostly delivered by 2027. With Phase II, datacenters are expected to be around 20% of the GAV. This could be achieved before 2030. Part of Phase I's around 20 MW of installed capacity should be delivered soon and start to earn rents.

Bottom Line

In terms of impacts from the Trump administration, the only thing really mentioned is that there was a dial-back of plans to invest in the datacenter footprint in Portugal due to some possible consequences from changes expected from the Trump administration of the AI Diffusion Act. However, it didn't seem to warrant a separate release, with plans instead to increase investments in Spain instead to offset the declines in Portugal. Ultimately, it's more of a shift in timelines and less a determined reduction in how much the company plans to invest overall, which is good to hear because there are some scale economies when it comes to how a datacenter is supposed to use the provided infrastructure.

Merlin remains alright. We don't think the 20% upside on the NAV discount is enough to be interested because it also doesn't take into account tax effects. But in general, we think that the development return on investments in these developments in the datacenter business in particular should yield overall GAV gain and, of course, we should see topline growth as these projects become delivered. A lot of the capacity that is in the process of being built is already pre-let, so we can be confident in at least several points of topline growth driven just by datacenter.
